packs.name: Getting Started
packs.description: Your first challenges with SketchBot. Learn how the robot moves, places lines, and reads the paper.

challenges.title: First Line
challenges.subtitle: Make the robot draw its first mark
challenges.description: Guide SketchBot to draw a single straight line across the paper. Simple — but it's the building block of everything.
learning_objectives: Understand how SketchBot reads the paper using markers
Learn how coordinates map to the physical page
Successfully complete a robot drawing action

steps.tutor_message: Hi! I'm Sketch. Before I can draw, I need to see the paper. Can you make sure all four corner markers are visible to the camera?
steps.student_prompt: Make sure all four AprilTag markers are in the camera view.

steps.tutor_message: Those corner markers are like a map for me — they tell me exactly where the paper is, even if someone bumps the table. Pretty clever, right?
steps.reflection_question: Why do you think I need to know where all four corners are, and not just one?

steps.tutor_message: Let's go! I'm going to draw a straight line across the middle of the page. Watch closely — you'll see my pen move in real time.

payload.prompt: a single horizontal straight line across the center of the page

steps.tutor_message: We did it! That's your first SketchBot drawing. Notice how the line went edge-to-edge? That's because I measured the whole page first.
steps.reflection_question: If you tilted the paper slightly before I drew, what do you think would happen to the line?

challenges.title: Shape Up
challenges.subtitle: Draw a perfect square
challenges.description: Guide SketchBot to draw a square. Discover what makes a shape 'perfect' and why right angles matter.
learning_objectives: Define what makes a square (equal sides, right angles)
Observe how robots use coordinates to draw geometric shapes
Connect geometric concepts to physical output

steps.tutor_message: Quick question before we draw — how many sides does a square have? And what's special about its corners?
steps.reflection_question: What's the difference between a square and a rectangle?

steps.tutor_message: Perfect thinking! Now watch — I'm going to draw a square in the center of the page. I'll move in four directions: right, down, left, up.

payload.prompt: a single centered square with equal sides

steps.tutor_message: Take a ruler and measure two sides of my square. Are they the same length? This is how you know the robot is working correctly!
steps.student_prompt: Measure two sides of the square with a ruler and compare.

steps.tutor_message: Great work! What did you find? A square's sides should all be within a millimeter of each other. Small errors creep in because I'm a physical robot — and that's totally okay!
steps.reflection_question: Why might a robot's square not be perfectly precise? What could cause tiny errors?

completion_badge.name: Shape Maker
completion_badge.description: Drew a perfect square with SketchBot

packs.name: Math Through Art
packs.description: Discover the hidden math in beautiful patterns. These challenges connect geometry, algebra, and number theory to real drawings.

challenges.title: Nature's Secret Pattern
challenges.subtitle: The Fibonacci spiral
challenges.description: A number pattern from the 13th century shows up in sunflowers, seashells, and galaxies. Today SketchBot will draw it.
learning_objectives: Understand the Fibonacci sequence and how it grows
Connect the number sequence to geometric squares and spirals
Observe the golden ratio in nature and art

steps.tutor_message: Here's a mystery: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21... what's the pattern? Each number is the sum of the two before it. This is called the Fibonacci sequence.
steps.student_prompt: What comes next in the sequence: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, ___?
steps.reflection_question: Try to guess: what do you think this number pattern has to do with drawing?

steps.tutor_message: Watch — I'm going to draw squares whose sides match the Fibonacci numbers: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8... each new square fits perfectly next to the last ones.

payload.prompt: Fibonacci rectangle grid: nested squares with side lengths 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8 arranged in the Fibonacci tiling pattern

steps.tutor_message: Now the magic moment — I'll draw a curve through each square's corner. That curve is the Fibonacci spiral, and it appears everywhere in nature.

payload.prompt: Fibonacci spiral: quarter-circle arcs connecting the corners of Fibonacci tiled squares, creating the golden spiral

steps.tutor_message: This exact spiral shows up in sunflower seeds, nautilus shells, pinecones, and even spiral galaxies. Math isn't just on paper — it's in everything.
steps.reflection_question: Can you think of something in nature that spirals outward? Why might a spiral be a good shape for a growing shell?

completion_badge.name: Nature's Mathematician
completion_badge.description: Discovered the Fibonacci spiral with SketchBot

challenges.title: Mirror, Mirror
challenges.subtitle: Lines of symmetry
challenges.description: Explore how shapes can be folded perfectly in half. SketchBot will draw symmetrical patterns and you'll discover the hidden rules.
learning_objectives: Identify lines of symmetry in shapes
Understand reflective symmetry vs rotational symmetry

steps.tutor_message: Imagine you could fold a shape perfectly in half and both sides match exactly. That fold line is called a line of symmetry. A circle has infinite of them — how many does a square have?
steps.student_prompt: Think about a square: how many ways can you fold it so both halves match perfectly?

steps.tutor_message: Let me draw something with perfect symmetry. Watch both sides of the drawing — they should be mirror images of each other.

payload.prompt: a simple symmetrical butterfly outline with a clear vertical line of symmetry

steps.tutor_message: Fold the paper along the butterfly's center line. Do the wings line up? That's symmetry you can physically test!
steps.student_prompt: Fold the paper along the center line of the drawing and check if both sides match.

steps.tutor_message: Your turn to design! Tell me something that has symmetry — like a face, a star, or a snowflake — and I'll draw it.
steps.student_prompt: Type your idea for a symmetrical drawing below.

completion_badge.name: Symmetry Seeker
completion_badge.description: Explored lines of symmetry with SketchBot

packs.name: Creative Studio
packs.description: Open-ended creative challenges. No right answers — just exploration, expression, and learning through making.

challenges.title: Robot Self-Portrait
challenges.subtitle: How does a robot see itself?
challenges.description: Ask SketchBot to draw its own self-portrait. Then discuss what a robot 'sees' versus what it 'thinks' it looks like.
learning_objectives: Explore the difference between AI-generated imagery and physical reality
Discuss how AI 'imagines' things it cannot directly perceive
Practice critiquing and giving feedback to an AI system

steps.tutor_message: Here's a fun question: I can draw anything you describe. But what if I try to draw *myself*? I've never seen my own body — only the paper in front of me. What do you think I'll come up with?
steps.reflection_question: What do you think SketchBot looks like? Describe it in a few words.

steps.tutor_message: Okay — here's my best guess at what I look like! Remember, I've only ever seen paper and ink...

payload.prompt: a robot self-portrait: a simple friendly drawing robot with wheels, a pen arm, and a camera eye, seen from the side

steps.tutor_message: What do you think? Does it look like me? Compare my drawing to what you see in the room. I'm curious what I got wrong!
steps.student_prompt: Compare SketchBot's self-portrait to the real robot. What's accurate? What's missing or different?
steps.reflection_question: What would you add or change to make this portrait more accurate?

steps.tutor_message: Now it's your turn — draw *me* on a piece of paper while I draw! Compare our versions when we're both done.
steps.student_prompt: Draw SketchBot on paper while it draws you. Compare results!

completion_badge.name: Art Critic
completion_badge.description: Explored AI imagination through robot self-portraiture
